items.title: Eight arrested after alleged plot to smuggle drugs, contraband into Halifax County Detention Center
items.summary_raw: Ten identified, eight arrested after an alleged plot to smuggle drugs and contraband into the Halifax County Detention Center, according to the Halifax County Sheriff's Office.

items.title: Ask the Meteorologist: How does current storm in the Gulf differ from Bertha of 1996?
items.summary_raw: A tropical depression in the Gulf will soon become Tropical Storm Bertha. How does this storm differ for us from what Hurricane Bertha brought in 1996?

items.title: The Galaxy Card Is Samsung’s Answer to the Apple Card
items.summary_raw: Directly added to your Samsung Wallet account, it’s yet another cash-back credit card, this time tailored for Samsung stans.

items.title: With just a week left until August recess, Congress faces long to-do list
items.summary_raw: Before the House adjourns for its August recess, House Speaker Mike Johnson wants to advance more defense funding, the president's election integrity bill and a stock trading ban for lawmakers.

items.title: At Mississippi funeral, civil rights leaders call for justice for Nolan Wells, 18
items.summary_raw: The parents of Nolan Wells, who was found dead on Horn Island in Mississippi, will hold a funeral for the 19-year-old on Monday. Wells was with friends for the Fourth of July and did not return home.

items.title: Trump imposes 50% tariff on Canadian imports
items.summary_raw: The duties mark a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours.

items.title: Scientists detect hidden skin damage before it becomes visible
items.summary_raw: Scientists have discovered a hidden warning sign of skin damage that appears before collagen fibers visibly thin, break, or lose their connections. Using advanced light-based techniques, the team found that collagen’s carefully organized molecular “handedness” can collapse even while the tissue still appears structurally intact and contains the same amount of collagen. This suggests that skin may begin deteriorating at a much deeper level long before conventional imaging can detect a problem.

items.title: Driver in deadly accident operated his tow truck for 14 months without a license
items.summary_raw: WRAL Investigates has learned the man who investigators say caused a deadly accident has a history of driving while impaired, and was operating his tow truck company for 14 months without a valid license.

items.title: How congress’s latest push to end the twice-yearly clock change could succeed
items.summary_raw: Congress has tried and failed before to standardize on daylight saving time, but bi partisan support of the improved bill has sparked cautious optimism that falling back and springing forward might no longer be necessary.

items.title: The FDA Says It Didn't Apologize to Supplier Linked to Diarrhea Outbreak, Actually
items.summary_raw: The agency stressed that the July 17 voluntary recall from Taylor Farms still stands. The supplier has been linked to the ongoing cyclospora outbreak.

items.title: Republican strategist on the hurdles the GOP faces to pass reconciliation bill
items.summary_raw: NPR's Leila Fadel speaks with Republican strategist Rob Burgess about Republican efforts to move forward with a $95 billion party-line budget reconciliation bill.

items.title: What led to the arrests of Andrew and Tristan Tate
items.summary_raw: Andrew Tate and his brother Tristan were arrested in Miami and now face extradition. New Yorker reporter Heidi Blake talks about the new rape and human trafficking charges from British prosecutors.

items.title: Yemen's Houthis announce 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia
items.summary_raw: The Houthis say the embargo begins immediately and is a response to a Saudi blockade of ports and airports in Yemen.

items.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
items.summary_raw: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach treetops, frighten predators, or impress potential mates.

items.title: Cary reviews town-issued credit card rules after auditor's report
items.summary_raw: A total of 828 Cary employees had a town-issued credit card, also known as a purchasing card or P-Card, as of November 2025. Employees charged more than $24 million to the cards over a two-year period.

items.title: Fans Flocked to World Cup Host Cities—Causing 80 Percent of the Tournament’s Emissions
items.summary_raw: The latest estimates reveal that the majority of greenhouse gas emissions from large-scale events such as the FIFA World Cup stem from spectators traveling to host cities.

items.title: Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner
items.summary_raw: Maine Democrats appear to be settling on a frontrunner to replace former Senate candidate Graham Platner in a rushed process to take on Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

items.title: 'Adams Parachute' couldn't save his son, but a grieving father hopes it will help others with addiction
items.summary_raw: A father who lost his son to addiction found healing through fly fishing's meditative nature, now he shares it with people recovering from addiction.

items.title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
items.summary_raw: The US says the attacks were aimed at degrading Iran's capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Earth’s waters are quietly running out of oxygen, scientists warn
items.summary_raw: Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, including warming, pollution, biodiversity loss, and ocean acidification.

items.title: Why one North Carolina county said yes to Amazon's $10 billion data center
items.summary_raw: Water, power and jobs all factored into Richmond County's decision to welcome Amazon's $10 billion data center while other communities said no.

items.title: Even Mild Sleep Deprivation May Lead to Weight Gain
items.summary_raw: A recent study suggests that not getting enough sleep increases the risk of weight gain, and thus of conditions like heart disease and type 2 diabetes.

items.title: Georgia official discusses Trump's claims about election security
items.summary_raw: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe talks with Gabriel Sterling, of the Georgia Secretary of State's Office, about President Trump's claims about election security.

items.title: 10 things that wowed us at the 2026 World Cup
items.summary_raw: The United States, Mexico, and Canada co-hosted the nearly six-week tournament. NPR's team of journalists who covered the World Cup reflects on the experience.

items.title: Notorious drug cartel leader Ismael Zambada sentenced to life in US prison
items.summary_raw: Known as "El Mayo", the co-founder the Sinaloa Cartel was also ordered to pay $15bn after he admitted to sending vast amounts of drugs to the US.

items.title: Alzheimer’s breakthrough: Scientists restore two hours of sleep without clearing brain plaques
items.summary_raw: Researchers have uncovered a surprising culprit behind sleep loss in Alzheimer’s disease: the brain’s own immune cells. In mice with amyloid plaques, overactive microglia triggered inflammation that kept the brain from getting enough deep, restorative sleep. Temporarily removing most of these cells restored more than two hours of sleep per day, even though the plaques remained unchanged.

items.title: Fact-check: Trump administration says 250,000 noncitizens are registered to vote in 4 states
items.summary_raw: In a release of documents alongside President Donald Trump’s July 16 address, the Department of Homeland Security said “over 250,000 non-citizens are illegally registered to vote in just the four states."

items.title: The 35 Best Board Games for Family Game Night
items.summary_raw: From monsters to kittens to strategy games, these sets will liven things up on nights when everyone is tired of screens.

items.title: Trump said the election system is broken. What do swing voters make of the claim?
items.summary_raw: What did some swing state voters think of President Trump's primetime speech on elections?

items.title: India's Gen Z 'Cockroach People's Party' started as satire but is serious about change
items.summary_raw: They call themselves the "cockroaches" and they're on the march — demanding action against corruption, amid a shortage of opportunities for young people in India.

items.title: Merger deal between Paramount and Warner Bros paused by judge
items.summary_raw: US court temporarily stops Paramount and Warner Bros merging after 12 states launched legal action.

items.title: An ordinary laptop solved a problem thought to require a quantum computer
items.summary_raw: A quantum problem once described as impossible for classical computers has now been solved using relatively modest hardware. Researchers used tensor networks to compress the overwhelming wave function created by hundreds of entangled qubits, allowing some calculations to run on a laptop. Their results matched both theoretical predictions and simulations performed with a quantum computer. The method could open new paths for exploring quantum dynamics and materials.

items.title: Rocky Mount resumes utility disconnections, fees after months of exceptions due to billing issue
items.summary_raw: The City of Rocky Mount has resumed shutting off utilities and charging late fees after months of exceptions due to a utility billing issue that led to back bills.

items.title: A Beginner’s Guide to Growing Mushrooms at Home (2026)
items.summary_raw: From countertop kits to a bathroom bucket full of straw and beyond, I spent a year testing popular mushroom-growing methods. Here’s what fruited—and what just grew mold.

items.title: $600,000 for a presidential pardon? Inside the growing industry of clemency lobbying
items.summary_raw: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe talks with NOTUS reporter Jasmine Wright about the growing lobbying industry to get presidential pardons under the second Trump administration.

items.title: How the Trump administration's new rules could affect international students
items.summary_raw: International students could once stay as long as they needed to complete their college degrees, but that's changing, and the restrictions could cut into college budgets.

items.title: Tate brothers appear in US court for extradition proceedings
items.summary_raw: They were arrested on Saturday after the UK announced dozens of new charges, including rape and sex trafficking, which they deny.

items.title: Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
items.summary_raw: A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.

items.title: Audit: Zebulon lacked proper financial oversight, overspent from general fund
items.summary_raw: The town of Zebulon lacked internal controls to effectively manage its finances, according to an audit released on Monday by the State Auditor's Office.

items.title: An Ebike Company Was Sued for Misleading Info on Safety. It Points to a Big Problem
items.summary_raw: Several Chinese businesses associated with the ebike brand Aipas settled a lawsuit with Amazon and the safety certification company UL in July.

items.title: Politics chat: How much faith can you have in the country's election systems?
items.summary_raw: President Trump and Secretary of Homeland Security Markwayne Mullin make claims about the vulnerability of election systems in the U.S., but how true are those claims?

items.title: New federal law could bring running water to tribal homes, but states have concerns
items.summary_raw: More than 10,000 homes on Southwestern Indian reservations lack running water. This despite tribes substantial water rights. A new solution is being held up as states fight over the Colorado River.

items.title: 'If only people knew what a tick could do' - the deadly meat allergy caused by tick bites
items.summary_raw: Mammalian meat allergy is a growing problem along Australia's east coast - and it's caused by tick bites.

items.title: UCLA scientists develop mineral sunscreen without the chalky white cast
items.summary_raw: Scientists have redesigned zinc oxide particles to create a mineral sunscreen that protects against ultraviolet radiation without leaving such a noticeable white cast. The SPF 30 formula could make sunscreen easier and more appealing to use across a wider range of skin tones.

items.title: Small plane damaged during landing at RDU; no injuries reported
items.summary_raw: Small-engine plane was damaged when landing at RDU. Officials say one person was on board and uninjured.

items.title: The ACLU Is Arming Lawyers to Expose State Surveillance Secrets
items.summary_raw: A new toolkit for attorneys in Massachusetts targets the technologies police use—and conceal—to build criminal cases, from facial recognition to AI-written police reports.

items.title: Cuban artist and dissident exiled from country after 5 years in prison arrives in US
items.summary_raw: A famous Cuban dissident artist and musician, Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara, arrived in Miami on Saturday after being released from a five-year prison sentence on the condition that he leave his country.

items.title: America's 250th carries somber note for forcibly displaced Indigenous groups
items.summary_raw: Every couple of years members of the Eastern Shawnee Tribe of Oklahoma travel back to visit ancestral and historic sites located in the Midwest. Members say it's especially poignant as the country celebrates the 250th year of American independence.

items.title: ‘All I think about is her’: Families demand answers as Nigeria's boat disasters keep happening
items.summary_raw: Hundreds of lives are lost each year in Nigeria as a result of boat accidents yet nothing seems to change.

items.title: Scientists found two powerful new ways to destroy forever chemicals
items.summary_raw: Scientists are testing two promising ways to destroy PFAS, the stubborn “forever chemicals” that can accumulate in water and resist normal treatment. One method uses collapsing vapor bubbles to generate extreme heat and reactive molecules, while the other uses cold plasma and rising gas bubbles to pull PFAS to the surface and break them apart.

items.title: Rainbow Snake population declining; public asked to report sightings
items.summary_raw: The NC Wildlife Resources Commission is asking the public to report Rainbow Snake sightings in an effort to protect the declining population in the state.

items.title: Elite Young Runners Are Becoming Freakishly Fast. Welcome to ‘Trackflation’
items.summary_raw: Four-minute miles are almost routine. Middle schoolers are beating college runners. Inside the youth track boom that’s turning kids and teens into speed machines.

items.title: Week in Politics: Trump's speech; midterm elections; Todd Blanche and Jay Clayton
items.summary_raw: Three months from the November midterms, President Trump is drawing attention to issues that are not top priorities for voters.

items.title: A cavalcade of international stars headlined the World Cup's first-ever halftime final show
items.summary_raw: With President Trump in attendance, an all-superstar lineup that included Madonna, BTS, Justin Bieber and Burna Boy felt a little more political than it might have otherwise.

items.title: Police block thousands of 'cockroach' protesters marching on India's parliament
items.summary_raw: Some protesters demanding education reforms said they were injured in clashes with police who banned the march.

items.title: New AI blood test predicts heart disease 15 years early
items.summary_raw: A new AI-powered blood test could give people a remarkably early warning of serious heart and circulation problems. Developed by researchers at the University of Hong Kong, CardiOmicScore analyzes thousands of proteins and metabolites to estimate the risk of six major cardiovascular diseases, including heart attack, stroke, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation. Unlike genetic risk scores, which remain fixed throughout life, the system captures biological changes linked to a person’s current health, lifestyle, and environment.

items.title: Endangered Indiana bat captured in NC for first time since 2015
items.summary_raw: Biologists with the North Carolina Wildlife Resources Commission discovered an endangered bat species that had not been captured in the state since 2015.

items.title: Apps Marketed to US Troops Are Shipping Chinese and Russian Code
items.summary_raw: A first-of-its-kind analysis found more than one in eight apps built for US service members carried foreign code—some from firms in nations the Pentagon designates as adversaries.

items.title: MLS works to bring in a new generation of fans as the World Cup comes to an end
items.summary_raw: In 1994, the last men's World Cup the U.S. hosted sparked soccer fever, from youth leagues to TV broadcasts to the formation of MLS. Now, can MLS harness this World Cup for a new generation of fans?

items.title: Ten killed as Russian attacks on merchant ships in Black Sea intensify
items.summary_raw: Russian strikes on the Odesa region have killed 28 people in July alone, local authorities say.

items.title: A tiny universe in a bottle reveals clues to the origins of life
items.summary_raw: Researchers have created cosmic dust from scratch by recreating space-like conditions inside glass tubes. The dust contains complex carbon-rich molecules built from elements essential to life and produces infrared signals similar to real material found in space. By studying these laboratory samples, scientists can explore how organic chemistry unfolds around stars and how comets, asteroids and meteorites may have carried those ingredients to Earth.

items.title: Durham schools remove produce from cafeterias amid cyclospora risk as year-round students return
items.summary_raw: Durham Public Schools will welcome back 4,000 year-round students on Monday with some big changes in place for school lunches amid the risk for cyclospora.

items.title: Best Power Banks (2026): My Picks After Testing Over 100
items.summary_raw: Keep your phone, laptop, handheld gaming console, and other electronics running with these travel-friendly power banks.

items.title: Newcastle, Liverpool and England legend Keegan dies
items.summary_raw: Former England captain and manager Kevin Keegan dies at the age of 75, seven weeks after he revealed he had been diagnosed with stage four cancer.

items.title: The mysterious hum heard around the world may finally have an explanation
items.summary_raw: The mysterious sound known as The Hum has been reported worldwide for decades, yet its source often remains impossible to find. Researchers tested whether people who hear it have exceptional low-frequency hearing or measurable sounds produced inside their ears, but neither explanation fit most participants. The strongest possibility is that many cases involve low-frequency tinnitus, even though the sound seems completely external.

items.title: Tow truck driver charged with DWI in deadly motorcycle crash gets $2M bond
items.summary_raw: A tow truck driver charged with DWI in a crash that killed a motorcyclist and injured three others had his bond increased to $2 million.

items.title: How to Disable Music Videos in Spotify
items.summary_raw: A recent update made video playback the default for songs and podcasts with a visual component. If you just want to listen to music—and save your data and battery—follow these tips.

items.title: Hamas names Khalil al-Hayya as new overall leader
items.summary_raw: The Palestinian armed group's Qatar-based chief negotiator replaces Yahya Sinwar, who was killed by Israeli forces in Gaza in 2024.

items.title: Coffee may help the body fight stress and aging through a hidden cellular switch
items.summary_raw: Scientists may have uncovered one reason coffee is repeatedly associated with healthier aging and lower disease risk. Compounds in coffee appear to activate NR4A1, a receptor that helps protect cells from stress, inflammation, and damage. These protective effects disappeared when the receptor was removed, strengthening the connection. Surprisingly, plant-based compounds such as caffeic acid seemed far more active than caffeine itself.

items.title: The 10 Best Electrolyte Powders (We Tested Over 20)
items.summary_raw: Get those lost minerals back with the help of our top electrolyte powders, tablets, drops, and chews for athletes, partiers, and everyone in between.

items.title: Lamine Yamal celebrates World Cup win with little brother who charmed fans
items.summary_raw: The three-year-old brother of Yamal has been hailed one of Spain's World Cup breakout stars by fans

items.title: Scientists just discovered a lost branch of Australia’s marsupials
items.summary_raw: Fossils from Queensland suggest a newly recognized marsupial order may have survived in Australia for around 35 million years, rewriting part of the story of how the continent's unique mammals evolved. The discovery challenges the idea that Australia's marsupials all came from a single straightforward ancestral lineage.

items.title: The Search for Another Earth-Like Planet Just Took a Big Step Forward
items.summary_raw: Astronomers have discovered a rocky exoplanet that has an atmosphere and sits within the habitable zone of a solar system.

items.title: Rare identical quadruplets born in Australia
items.summary_raw: The parents of the four naturally-conceived girls were taken by "immense surprise" with the pregnancy, the hospital said.

items.title: Scientists identify the rare meteorite that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago
items.summary_raw: The asteroid that wiped out the dinosaurs was likely an exceptionally rare CO chondrite from a distant region of the solar system. Its unusual chemistry suggests that planet-cooling dust and debris, rather than sulfur inside the asteroid, may have delivered the deadliest blow.

items.title: FIFA’s World Cup Halftime Show Puts American-Style Spectacle on the World Stage
items.summary_raw: Organizers want the show to be the most-viewed halftime show in history. Having Shakira, Justin Bieber, Coldplay, and BTS will surely help—but do soccer fans want it?

items.title: Why are thousands of people protesting in Delhi?
items.summary_raw: Thousands of mostly young people tried to march on India's parliament to demand changes to how the education system is run.

items.title: Common antidepressant may ease long COVID’s crushing fatigue
items.summary_raw: A low-cost antidepressant may offer new hope for people struggling with long COVID fatigue. In a randomized clinical trial involving 399 adults, fluvoxamine significantly reduced fatigue and improved quality of life compared with a placebo, making it one of the first medications to show meaningful benefits for this disabling condition.

items.title: How To Watch the 2026 FIFA World Cup Finals: Spain vs. Argentina
items.summary_raw: The end of the FIFA Men’s World Cup is nigh. Here’s how to watch the final games and the first ever World Cup halftime show.

items.title: Utah firefighter and four family members killed in flash flooding
items.summary_raw: A husband, wife and their three sons died - meaning that only a daughter who did not join the outing is still alive.
